data.title: Equity Outlook Q2 2026: Global Growth Holds Firm As Geopolitical Risk Simmers
data.description:
By Amr Hanafy

Persistent Macro Tailwinds and Broadening Earnings Growth Keep Us Constructive on Risk Assets Through the Fog of War

“We remain encouraged by an ongoing global industrial rebound, broadening of capex beyond tech, and improving earnings growth. Our base case implies the U.S./Iran conflict de-escalates within the next few weeks.

Yet we are by no means complacent: Potential protracted conflict in Iran, softening cyclical employment and fading liquidity momentum represent meaningful near-term risks to global equity markets.”

Part 1: The Macro Story Remains Intact

  • Global industrial activity continues to expand
  • Global credit remains above pre-pandemic trend
  • U.S. labor conditions remain healthy
  • Larger U.S. tax refunds may support discretionary spending

Part 2: Markets Continue to Broaden

  • Capex growth is broadening beyond tech
  • EPS growth for the median stock continues to improve
  • Rotation toward value stocks and small caps may resume as growth continues to rebound
  • Institutional equity positioning could rise strongly as volatility subsides

Part 3: Risks to Our Thesis

  • Higher energy prices could have a non-linear negative impact on global growth and inflation
  • An oil shock could erode consumers’ real purchasing power just as their savings buffers have thinned
  • A meaningful market pullback could trigger a reverse wealth effect

Part 4: Portfolio Considerations

  • Regions: Remain overweight Japan and EM; upgrade U.S. to overweight; downgrade Europe to underweight
  • U.S. sectors: Maintain overweight Energy and Technology (a cyclical/growth barbell)
  • U.S. sizes and styles: Equal weight large caps vs. small caps, and value vs. growth

Part 1: The Macro Story Remains Intact

Despite ongoing geopolitical conflicts and growing concern about near-term market volatility, we believe the fundamental case for global economic growth remains intact and broadly supportive of risk assets. After a 3-year global manufacturing slump, a rising number of countries have been reporting 50+ PMIs (top chart) and global credit continues to run above its pre-pandemic trend (bottom chart).

Multiple indicators suggest to us that the industrial rebound is broadening beyond the U.S. technology sector. We believe new orders less inventories is pointing toward a headline PMI index in the mid-to-high 50s (top chart) and that the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) could drive meaningful capex growth, especially in the first half of 2026 (bottom chart).

Growth in average weekly payrolls (a proxy for nominal income and spending) remains resilient and close to its average pre-pandemic level (top chart). We believe payroll growth will likely support U.S. consumption, which accounts for roughly 70% of U.S. GDP. While the Labor Market Conditions Index (a comprehensive indicator) has softened, overall labor conditions remain healthy (bottom left) and increased tax refunds due to the OBBBA could further support near-term discretionary spending (bottom right).

Part 2: Markets Continue to Broaden

While U.S. hyper-scalers continue to drive overall capex investment, the rest of the economy has begun to follow suit. Capex growth within the rest of the S&P 500 has ticked up and is beginning to broaden out (top chart). Analysts are now revising capex estimates higher (bottom chart).

We believe improving corporate fundamentals will continue to drive the broadening-out trade: The median stock across the Russell 3000 Index (which includes small-cap, mid-cap and large-cap names) has seen steady improvement in EPS growth over the last eight quarters.

Thus far in 2026, we have seen a consistent pattern across the entire investment style box, with value stocks and small caps outperforming growth stocks and large caps. After a pause during the Iran conflict, we believe these recent dynamics could resume and have the potential to endure over the near to mid term.

Rising oil prices have rattled energy-dependent equity markets, such as Japan, Korea, and the eurozone, and threaten to thwart the broader global industrial recovery (top chart). However, history suggests to us that sell-offs driven by geopolitical events can be relatively short-lived and be followed by sharp rebounds(bottom chart). Assuming the conflict subsides within the next few weeks, we believe markets with relatively solid fundamentals could rerate on rebounding investor sentiment.

Institutional investors have reduced exposure to risk assets in the wake of the Iran conflict. Equity positioning has fallen from the 70th percentile level to the 27th percentile level (top chart); meanwhile, allocations to safer, cash-equivalent vehicles have sharply increased (bottom chart). We believe this setup offers more room for upside to risk assets once the fog of war begins to clear.

Part 3: Key Risks to Our Thesis

History suggests to us that sharp and swift increases in the price of oil—a key input in many energy and industrial markets—tend to coincide with recessions (top chart). Our base case assumes that the U.S./Iran war de-escalates within the next few weeks.

Yet if the recently reopened Strait of Hormuz once again becomes a point of contention, we fear further interruption in oil flows and supply chains could have non-linear negative impacts on global growth and inflation and negatively impact risk assets (bottom chart).

We believe that elevated energy prices could further pressure consumers’ discretionary spending when it is already in decline (top chart); meanwhile, overall consumer spending has been outpacing income growth for over a year, squeezing real purchasing power just as savings buffers have thinned.

And with households sitting at record equity allocations, we worry a meaningful market pullback could trigger a reverse wealth effect and potentially put additional pressure on discretionary spending (bottom chart).

Growth in cyclical employment—the more economically sensitive segment of the labor market—continues to decline, albeit at a slower pace (top chart). We believe broader deployment of AI could apply more pressure, though company announcements suggest that overall market conditions have been driving far more job cuts than deployment of AI (bottom chart). In short, AI’s impact on the labor market is visible, but still relatively muted.

Part 4: Portfolio Considerations

Regions: Remain overweight Japan and EM

We remain constructive on global equities, with overweights in both Japan and EM.

Both markets are inherently more cyclical and therefore more geared to global industrial production (top left chart).

Following a 3-year manufacturing slump, we believe these markets could outperform as global growth recovers.

We expect the USD to continue weakening as global growth rebounds, an environment that potentially bodes well for EM stocks outperforming those in developed markets (top right).

Finally, the share of global equity flows into ex-U.S. markets continues to increase (bottom).

Regions: Upgrade U.S. to Overweight

Overall, we believe the U.S. macro backdrop remains solid and able to weather the current energy shock better than other major economies. We expect fiscal and monetary policy, along with accelerating private-sector capex (increasingly driven by AI-related investment), will continue to support growth. Furthermore, the U.S. economy’s oil intensity has dropped significantly over the past 50 years: Just 0.3 barrels are consumed per $1,000 of GDP today, compared to 0.9 barrels during the 1973 oil embargo.

Meanwhile, liquidity has provided a timely cushion during the U.S./Iran conflict and continues to support financial conditions (top chart). And with valuations meaningfully de-rated, earnings and sales revisions still positive (bottom chart), and institutional positioning still relatively modest, we believe U.S. equities warrant a slight upgrade.

Regions: Downgrade Europe to Underweight

We believe the risk-reward outlook in European equities has turned unfavorable. Gas reserves in the Eurozone are well below average levels, amplifying the region’s exposure to a protracted energy-supply shock (top left chart) and potential inflation, prompting the ECB to tighten policy into economic weakness rather than strength. Meanwhile, real money supply growth—a leading indicator of economic activity—could already be peaking (top right).

Earnings growth in Europe is mainly a cyclical story: EPS growth for cyclical sectors in 2026 is expected to be 31% vs. just 5% for defensive sectors, with autos and banks (two key cyclical sectors) accounting for roughly half of total EPS growth (bottom chart). We fear a slowdown in manufacturing activity or ill-timed rate hikes could put the bulk of that cyclical equities growth at risk.

Size: Equal weight U.S. large caps vs. U.S. small caps

While we remain constructive on U.S. small caps, we believe the macro backdrop now favors high-quality U.S. large caps, especially amidst rising geopolitical risk and uncertainty.

The forward P/E on the S&P 500 Index has fallen 10% from its October 2025 peak on fears of AI disruption and the impacts of the conflict in Iran. Yet since the conflict began, forward earnings estimates among large caps have risen by 5%, with technology firms accounting for two-thirds of that increase (top chart).

Within the broader large-cap group, the Mag 7 now trade at 26x forward earnings. Relative to the S&P 493, that’s a level near the troughs of the late-2022 sell-off and Liberation Day (bottom chart), all while earnings growth for the group has remained resilient. From a risk-reward standpoint, we believe this setup could provide an attractive entry point.

Sectors: Energy

We believe the energy sector continues to offer a compelling combination of strong fundamentals, investment flows and relative valuations.

Relative performance of energy stocks tends to track the price of oil, which has spiked during the Iran conflict (top left chart). We believe the sector’s earnings estimates do not yet reflect potentially higher-for-longer oil prices (top right).

After years of outflows, investor sentiment is now beginning to turn, yet we believe positioning is still far from stretched (bottom left). And even after the recent rally, we think relative valuations remain attractive (bottom right).

Equity Recommendations

Disclosures

Index Definitions

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 U.S. stocks chosen for market size, liquidity and industry group representation. It is a market value-weighted index (stock price times number of shares outstanding), with each stock’s weight in the Index proportionate to its market value.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively higher price-to-book ratios, higher I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and higher sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The MSCI ACWI Index captures large and mid-cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (DM) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. DM countries include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the U.K. and the U.S. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates. With 2,837 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set.

The MSCI China Index captures large and mid-cap representation across China A shares, H shares, B shares, Red chips, P chips and foreign listings (e.g., ADRs). With 655 constituents, the index covers about 85% of this China equity universe.

The MSCI Japan Index is designed to measure the performance of the large and mid-cap segments of the Japanese market. With 203 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in Japan.

The MSCI Europe Index captures large and mid-cap representation across Developed Markets (DM) countries in Europe. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization across the European Developed Markets equity universe.

The STOXX 600 Index is a broad measure of the European equity market, consisting of 600 components that represent large-,mid-, and small-capitalization companies across 17 countries and 11 industries within Europe. It covers approximately 90% of the free-float market capitalization of the European stock market.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/27/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

data.title: HOOY: Forget Getting 108% Distributions From Robinhood
data.description:
Thesis

Covered call ETFs have been the rage in the past year with eye-popping distribution rates and return profiles that made investors salivate. They are not all the same, though, and they do not always work. In today's article we are going to explore the YieldMax offering in the space for the Robinhood (HOOD) stock and show investors why they are better off just buying the stock outright in this particular case.

What Are Covered Call ETFs Supposed to Do?

In an ideal structuring and volatility world, a covered call ETF purely serves as an instrument to extract dividends from an underlying index or stock. The 'perfect' covered call ETF has a total return profile very much correlating 1:1 with the underlying index or stock. Unfortunately, there is no such thing as a 'perfect' covered call ETF, although some of the ones pertaining to the S&P 500 Index (SPX) come close:

We have covered some of the names in this chart before, but the correlation is very high, both on the upside and on the downside. The NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) and Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) come close to being 'perfect'.

By now investors will probably have understood how the graph of a good covered call ETF should look. Also note that the more volatile a stock or index, the harder it is to create a covered call ETF.

Robinhood is a very popular stock with very high volatility. Given its popularity, ETF managers have created a covered call ETF for it, but it does not quite work. Let's see.

What Is HOOY Supposed To Do?

Let us start by looking at the fund objective as presented on their website:

The YieldMax® HOOD Option Income Strategy ETF (HOOY) is an actively managed ETF designed to generate weekly income by selling call spreads on Robinhood Markets Inc (HOOD). By writing call spreads, HOOY seeks to systematically harvest option premiums from HOOD’s volatility, striving to turn it into a weekly income stream while still maintaining participation in HOOD’s share price appreciation. HOOY offers a compelling way to potentially collect income and stay long HOOD.

Please note the fund objective is not to transform HOOD's capital gains into dividends but to harvest option premiums while 'maintaining participation' in HOOD's share price. So at least HOOY is honest about what it does and what it does not do.

The ETF aims to achieve its goal via a complex holdings structure that includes call spreads, a synthetic long via a short put position, and an upside trade via a long call position:

Note the various building blocks in the above table from Excel that includes the short put position (the 70 strike short put), the various call spreads, and the long call position with a May 2026 expiry and a 98 strike. We are not going to go into detail on whether this is a good build or not because the ETF is active, meaning that it can change its structure constantly. For such a fund with such a goal, what matters is the ultimate total return profile and whether that matches what the underlying does. Let's see if that is the case or not.

Does HOOY Do Its Job?

Ultimately, investors in HOOY should be interested if the fund does its job when a total return is concerned. The answer, however, is not very encouraging:

In the past year, the basis between the YieldMax HOOD Option Income Strategy ETF (HOOY) and the outright equity Robinhood Markets has been very high. While the difference in total returns is now a 'mere' 20%, late last year that figure was as high as 60%.

Investors should always, always look at total returns versus the underlying equity because as an investor in HOOY, you retain the same downside as the outright stock, but not the same upside. The higher the differential in good times, the worse the covered call fund. High-beta names like HOOD are very difficult to insert in the covered call fund format because they experience periods of explosive growth in price, which is something that covered call funds struggle with. As a rule of thumb, the higher the beta for an equity, the harder it is for a covered call fund to work properly.

As an Investor You Retain All the Downside

Do not let anybody fool you about the single-name covered call funds - as an investor here, you retain the full downside of the underlying stock:

In the above chart, we are comparing HOOD with HOOY and another new entrant in the covered call space, namely the REX HOOD Growth & Income ETF (HOII). As you can see, in the past six months, HOOD has lost -38%, while HOOY is down -31%. The downside is almost identical, with a slight outperformance by HOOY. In a bear market, do expect HOOY holders to very much mimic the outright stock downside, with a slight edge from monetizing volatility via options selling.

So Why Sell HOOY? Because You Are Better Off on Your Own

In our view, the selling point for covered call funds is to do well and in a systematic fashion something that as investors we have a harder time - namely selling small chunks of a position to monetize gains and create dividends. It is not very hard to do for high beta names: let us assume we buy $100 of HOOD and it goes to $200 at the end of the year. As investors, we can simply sell half of the position and thus create a 'dividend' of 100% at the end of the year. It is not hard to do. It becomes more tricky with names that move up by +7% or +12% on the year. Very often selling at the end of the year is not the best way to do it, but selling small chunks monthly works better. And this is a problem for retail investors who do not have the time or expertise to manage this. Hence the covered call funds.

However, given its large basis to the underlying stock, HOOY is a bad proxy for what it is supposed to do. Moreover, the structuring is lacking, with the ETF generating high distributions but leaving a massive capital loss in your account:

In the past year, the ETF is down -40% on a price basis, although on a total return basis, it is positive. Why? Because the structure is not ideal for a high-beta name like HOOD, and the distributions gained are composed of a significant amount of return of capital:

The 108% distribution rate on the fund's website is not real, and it represents the product of the fund structure via options and the high beta nature of the underlying stock. We do not like this. We do not like to have a -40% 'hole' in our brokerage account and a massive difference to the HOOD total return. We are of the opinion that for a high-beta name like this one, a retail investor is better served by buying the stock outright and revisiting the position monthly or quarterly and making stock sales to extract the necessary capital gains when they occur. We are therefore a 'Sell' for HOOY because it is a high-expense fund (0.99% expense ratio) that does not really work well for a high-beta name like HOOD. Just buy HOOD outright and revisit the position.

Conclusion

HOOY is a single-stock YieldMax product that aims to capture the HOOD total returns and volatility. The ETF uses options to do that but has a very difficult time capturing the upside during aggressive upswings. HOOY also uses significant return of capital, with the fund down -40% in the past year on a price basis, although its total return is +36%. For a high-beta name like HOOD, we are of the opinion that an outright position managed actively is better than buying HOOY.

data.title: Pembina Pipeline: Limited Upside, But Still A Buy For Yield Investors
data.description:
Earlier in April, Pembina Pipeline (PBA) provided an update on its business outlook through 2030. I covered the stock in February, and since then the stock price has actually remained stable. That is not the price performance I am looking for in a buy-rated name, and I also noted that the stock price underperformed the broader financial markets. In this report, I discuss the growth outlook and update my expectations for Pembina.

Pembina Pipeline Targets Mid-Single-Digit CAGR on EBITDA

Pembina is targeting fee-based EBITDA to improve from $3.93 billion to $5.175 billion, or 32% growth, through 2030, equating to a CAGR of 5%-7% annually. Increased utilization of existing assets will grow 4% annually, projects under development will grow 6% annually with 7% for other projects. The main driver of total growth is higher volumes and sanctioned projects followed by projects under development. This should bring EBITDA per share to $8.25-$8.90. The company expects take-of-pay EBITDA contributions to be 65%-70% in 2030 compared to 75% in 2025, with 20% fee-based EBITDA compared to 15% today and 10%-15% commodity exposed compared to 10% today. I would rather have seen take-or-pay remaining at 75%, because it is equivalent to a capacity lease whether capacity is utilized or not. Fee-for-service is dependent on the volume flowing through the system providing volume upside opportunities but also leaves the company somewhat exposed to volume fluctuations.

The company is building its strategy from a 3Cs framework:

  1. Capture: Expanding in top-tier resource basins.
  2. Connect: Link hydrocarbons to domestic and global markets.
  3. Catalyze: Build solutions for new demand areas such as data centers.

The company is positioning itself for global energy demand growth as an integrated wellhead-to-market solutions provider in Canada, with Canada positing itself as an energy superpower.

Pembina Stock Has Limited Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Typically, I am looking for 10% upside for stocks, and for midstream companies, I am looking for at least 7% upside. Pembina is not the most attractive stock in that regard. Against expected earnings for 2027, the stock only offers 6% upside. The reason why it still scores a buy rating is because its annualized return potential towards 2028 still exceeds 7% per annum. The company is not high growth, with 5.6% annual growth through 2028 on sales and 3.7% on EBITDA, with Pembina’s guidance suggesting an acceleration in later years. Free cash flow is also expected to show only moderate growth due to an increase in capital expenditures. So, it is not odd that the stock does not show significant upside. We also note that the stock has a 10-year EV/EBITDA median of 14x, but it has not traded near those levels in recent years. That is why I am not using the company’s median EV/EBITDA to set a price target.

For companies such as Pembina, the appeal is not so necessarily in upside but in the dividend yield and the opportunity to build a strong yield on cost. We already note that by 2028, the yield on cost could start exceeding 5%. The company’s margins are expected to settle at 52% in the years ahead, with elevated capital expenditures expected for 2026 and 2027. The net debt leverage remains more than acceptable for a midstream company.

Conclusion: Not the Most Attractive Stock but Still a Buy

Pembina Pipeline is unlikely to be the most attractive midstream stock, with the company barely meeting the 7% annualized upside that I am looking for. However, the company guides for 5%-7% annual growth in EBITDA towards 2030, and that suggests that EBITDA contribution should substantially pick up after 2028. For now, I am maintaining my buy rating for Pembina, as I still do like the opportunity to build a strong yield on cost for the long term.

data.title: Compass, Inc.: Well Positioned To Emerge As A Dominant Exchange
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Third Avenue Small-Cap Value Fund Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter.

During the quarter, the Fund initiated a position in Compass, Inc. (COMP)

Compass, Inc. is a technology enabled residential real estate brokerage focused on the luxury and upper-middle segments of the U.S. housing market. The company operates a national platform that combines the nation's largest agent network with proprietary software tools designed to enhance productivity, client engagement and transaction efficiency. Under this business model, agents are independent contractors who generate commissions on the purchase and sale of a home and revenue is generated primarily from commissions on home sales.

U.S. existing home sales are at historically depressed levels, with U.S. residential transactions at their lowest levels on a per capita basis in 60 years. Compass has been pressured by a combination of higher mortgage rates, affordability challenges and constrained housing inventory, weighing on transaction volumes and commission revenue across the industry. At the same time, investor uncertainty has arisen around the long term economics of residential brokerage, fears of commission compression, heightened agent competition and potential technological disintermediation. On the other hand, Compass' recent transformational merger with Anywhere Real Estate (HOUS) creates the largest residential real estate platform globally, expands Compass's agent and brand footprint and diversifies revenue through recurrent high margin franchise, title, escrow, and relocation revenue streams.

We expect the industry will continue to move towards a structure in which there are several dominant platforms that effectively serve as exchanges for residential transactions. Compass is very well positioned to emerge as one of those dominant exchanges capitalizing upon the strength of the company's digital search platform combined with a leading selection of property listings facilitated by its increasing agent count. As transaction volumes eventually normalize, we believe Compass will achieve significant profitability, demonstrating the work that both standalone Compass and Anywhere had done pre-merger to achieve substantial cost savings in recent years. Given the inherent operating leverage of the business model, any increase in transaction volumes would meaningfully improve profitability.

Important Information

This publication does not constitute an offer or solicitation of any transaction in any securities. Any recommendation contained herein may not be suitable for all investors. Information contained in this publication has been obtained from sources we believe to be reliable, but cannot be guaranteed.The information in this portfolio manager letter represents the opinions of the portfolio manager(s) and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Views expressed are those of the portfolio manager(s) and may differ from those of other portfolio managers or of the firm as a whole. Also, please note that any discussion of the Fund's holdings, the Fund's performance, and the portfolio manager(s) views are as of March 31, 2026 (except as otherwise stated), and are subject to change without notice. Certain information contained in this letter constitutes "forward-looking statements," which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as "may," "will," "should," "expect," "anticipate," "project," "estimate," "intend," "continue" or "believe," or the negatives thereof (such as "may not," "should not," "are not expected to," etc.) or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results or the actual performance of any fund may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in any such forward-looking statement. Current performance results may be lower or higher than performance numbers quoted in certain letters to shareholders.Past performance is no guarantee of future results; returns include reinvestment of all distributions. The above represents past performance and current performance may be lower or higher than performance quoted above. Investment return and principal value fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. data.title: Flagstar Bank: Back-To-Back Profitability And Credit Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Flagstar Bank's (FLG) rally could be set to continue on the back of a second consecutive quarter of positive non-GAAP profits, a credit rating upgrade from Fitch, and a growing deposit base. FLG reported fiscal 2026 first-quarter revenue of $498 million, up 1.6% over its year-ago comp but a miss by $54.83 million on consensus estimates. Critically, the bank reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.04 that was in line with analyst consensus. This profit print was the main headline, as it was the second consecutive quarter of positive non-GAAP EPS. This has helped drive the commons up by 25% over the last 1 year. FLG is now trading at a roughly 11% discount to its tangible book value of $15.70 per share at the end of its first quarter and is materially lower than the all-time high before new management took over the bank in 2024 after a series of FDIC-insured bank failures.

FLG is focused on delivering across four areas as it continues to push to reduce its exposure to rent-stabilized apartments in New York City against the specter of a rent freeze. Delivery across these four verticals drove a credit upgrade to "BB" from Fitch Ratings, a single-notch upgrade and just one below an investment grade rating. The bank grew total deposits by $832 million during the first quarter, an expansion of around 1.3% sequentially to bring its total to $66.8 billion. Crucially, FLG has a well-diversified deposit base from a mix of products. The growth seen during the first quarter was driven by Commercial and Private Bank deposits of $461 million, with insured deposits forming 80% of total deposits, higher than its peer group average of 56%.

CRE Loan Exposure, EPS, and De-Risking

FLG's total commercial real estate ("CRE") portfolio had a balance of $34.3 billion as of the end of the first quarter. There has been a 28% reduction in this total since the end of FLG's fiscal 2023, with $1.1 billion in payoffs seen during the first quarter. The bank flagged that 42% of payoffs were substandard loans, with the CRE concentration ratio dropping to 367% as of the end of the first quarter. This was highlighted by Fitch as a reason for the upgrade. Bears would be right to flag that it's higher than peer regional banks and means elevated FLG exposure to an industry that has struggled since the pandemic. However, the ratio is down from 471% at the end of 2024. I last covered the ticker in October with a Hold rating, highlighting some risks to its multifamily exposure from pending rent freezes. FLG has since continued to de-risk its CRE portfolio.

FLG's multifamily exposure has constantly been flagged by bears as a reason to sell the bank, and the security had a short interest of 15% as of the end of March. The continued pace of multifamily loan runoff should see a balance, which stood at $26 billion at the end of the first quarter, likely end 2026 below $22 billion as $3.7 billion of multifamily loans come due for repayment this year. This repayment ladder will ramp up to $8.2 billion in 2027. Hence, this section of FLG's portfolio is set to markedly become less relevant to its financials, especially against the current 0.52% net charge-offs to average loan percentage. FLG's total loans on non-accrual status stood at $2.68 billion at the end of the first quarter, down around $323 million, or 11%, sequentially. While the net charge-offs to average loan percentage were up by 22 basis points from the prior quarter, this was mainly driven by a single relationship that was resolved during the quarter.

The 6.375% Series A Fixed-to-Floating Rate Preferreds (FLG.PR.A) and the 6.00% Bifurcated Option Note due 11/1/2051 (FLG.PR.U) have typically been my prudent choice for income following prior uncertainty around the commons, but the risk-reward paradigm has tilted more towards the commons despite the 7.4% current yield on the notes that are trading for a 20% discount to their $50 maturity value per note. The healthy trendline in non-GAAP EPS should continue through 2026, likely putting FLG in a position where some of the short interest will start to peel off. FLG does not pay a common share dividend, so the preferreds continue to be a healthy income choice.

Conclusion

FLG's intense push to de-risk its loan portfolio has driven two back-to-back quarters of positive earnings, with its credit rating upgraded and its revenue growing versus its year-ago comp. I am rating FLG as a Buy.

data.title: ASML: Strong Play On The AI Boom
data.description:
Earlier this month, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) reported better-than-expected earnings on booming AI chip demand, which indicated that suppliers like ASML (ASML) were also set for robust earnings reports. The Netherlands-based semiconductor equipment manufacturer indeed reported higher-than-expected earnings and revenues for Q1'26 amid growing demand for its lithography machines that are a vital component in the chip manufacturing process. With CapEx spending set to surge higher in 2026 -- amid an expected expansion in the total addressable market for AI chips, led by hyperscalers -- I believe ASML remains an attractive investment choice in the supplier market for investors that want to gain exposure to the server and chip industry.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of ASML a strong buy in October 2025 -- A Winner In The AI Arms Race -- in large part because the equipment manufacturer had strong implied operating margin upside in a market that was scrambling to get its hands on high-performing AI-optimized chips for Data Centers.

I like the investment setup for ASML and see growing demand for lithography systems, which are needed to manufacture semiconductors at scale. ASML raised its top line outlook for FY 2026 and is set to remain a beneficiary of the booming AI chip market. Since CapEx spending is set to go all but up this year, ASML's strong buy rating is confirmed.

ASML Surpassed Q1'26 Expectations

ASML reported higher-than-expected earnings and revenues for Q1'26 quarter on April 15, 2026, supporting an overall bullish investment outlook for both the industry and ASML: the equipment manufacturer had non-GAAP earnings of $8.44 per share, beating the consensus estimate by $0.63 per share. The top line came in at $10.4B and was $108M better than the average prediction.

ASML published a total top line figure of €8.8B for the first fiscal quarter, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 13%. This growth was driven primarily by a higher number of lithography machines and net system sales, which reached a total of €6.3B in Q1'26, highlighting 19% year-over-year growth.

Lithography machines that are needed by global fabs such as TSMC in their manufacturing processes for AI-optimized chips and red-hot demand for those systems underpinned ASML's strong financial results for the first quarter. Lithography machines are highly complex and extremely expensive -- often costing hundreds of millions of dollars -- in order to print patterns with precision at the nanometer scale. ASML is a leader in the market for ultraviolet lithography, with a monopoly on Extreme Ultraviolet lithography (EUV).

AI-driven chip demand remained extremely strong in the first-quarter -- as I explained in my TSMC work earlier this month, The Ultimate AI Growth Engine, which in turn also provided significant revenue and earnings tailwinds for ASML. Since fabs like TSMC are currently ordering both EUV and DUV (Deep Ultraviolet) systems in order to prepare for capacity expansions later in 2026, the demand situation for ASML is set to remain beneficial.

The semiconductor equipment manufacturer sold 79 high-performing lithography machines in the first quarter. Of those, 16 were EUV machines and represented a massive 66% share of all net system sales in Q1'26.

The order situation for ASML will remain highly beneficial also because CapEx estimates for Data Center spending continue to get revised to the upside. For the 2027 year, analysts expect that the server market will see approximately $800B in CapEx spending.

This spending is mainly driven by the five largest hyperscalers -- Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG), Apple (AAPL), and Amazon (AMZN) -- but neoclouds like Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV) are also seeing nice CapEx ramps in their businesses amid an acceleration of Cloud compute demand. Amazon alone said it will spend approximately $200B on AI investments just this year.

This attractive CapEx forecast, which should immediately translate into higher demand for AI-optimized chips and the equipment needed to manufacture them, is set to be a catalyst for ASML. In fact, because of this increasingly bullish demand outlook and growing order intake, the equipment manufacturer raised its revenue outlook from €34-39B to €36-40B.

In terms of earnings, ASML provided a solid profile as well: the equipment maker's gross margin and operating income saw double-digit growth year-over-year on an expansion in order volumes, and margins remained fairly steady: ASML's gross profit margins came in at 53%, showing a 0.8 PP Q/Q margin gain, while its operating income margin improved 0.7 PP Q/Q to 36%, the highest margin the equipment maker posted in a year.

€millions Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Y/Y Growth
Total Net Revenue 7,742 7,692 7,516 9,718 8,767 13%
Gross Profit 4,180 4,130 3,880 5,068 4,645 11%
Gross Margin (%) 54.0% 53.7% 51.6% 52.2% 53.0% -2%
Operating Income 2,738 2,664 2,468 3,431 3,158 15%
Operating Margin (%) 35.4% 34.6% 32.8% 35.3% 36.0% 2%

Source: Author

ASML's Valuation

ASML has one of the highest price-to-earnings ratios in the semiconductor market and is trading at an even higher valuation multiplier than NVIDIA (NVDA), a company that is almost entirely controlling the GPU market. Shares of ASML are currently priced at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 29.8X, which compares to a 3-year average P/E ratio of 27.8X.

Applied Materials (AMAT) and Lam Research (LRCX), which are considered competitors to ASML given their exposure to the same underlying growth trends in the semiconductor market, are trading at price-to-earnings ratios of 28.7X and 33.6X... leading to an industry group average P/E ratio of 30.7X.

A ~35X P/E ratio, to which ASML can easily revalue given its strong order backdrop, red-hot demand for lithography machines, and monopoly position in the EUV market, implies a fair value -- using a consensus FY 2027 EPS estimate of $47.37 -- of $1,659 per share. This stock price target translates to about 14% of additional upside potential for investors.

Risks With ASML

The biggest risk that I see for ASML is that the equipment maker could be subjected to tightening export controls for its semiconductor equipment. U.S. lawmakers recently proposed new legislation -- the so-called "MATCH Act" -- to restrict the firm's sales of less-advanced DUV machines to China. ASML generated 19% of its Q1'26 revenue mix from China, so the company has some considerable sales risk here. In anticipation of this regulation, management guided for its revenue percentage originating from China to drop to about 20% in FY 2026, down from 33% in the 2025 fiscal year.

Final Thoughts

ASML is a critical element in the semiconductor supply and value chain and key to servicing the market's growing demand for high-performing AI-optimized chips that are stacked in Data Centers. ASML is benefiting from growing hyperscaler-driven CapEx spending in the server market. Estimates for CapEx spending are rising, and major hyperscalers, like Amazon, are ramping up their AI projects aggressively. I like ASML as a long-term holding that is benefiting from an expansion in the total addressable market in the Data Center market and believe that the company's lithography machines will continue to fulfill a bottleneck function in the AI industry.

data.title: Qualys: Generative AI Agent Proliferation Drives Rising Demand
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from this fund letter.

During the quarter, the Fund initiated a position in Qualys, Inc.

Qualys, Inc (QLYS) is an established, growing, and highly profitable cybersecurity company and the largest of three leading specialized vendors of vulnerability management software. Qualys was among the first to build a cloud security platform and has continued to improve its offerings and applications. Its highly regarded software solutions, which identify and repair vulnerabilities to cyber threats within customer networks, are well utilized by a majority of Global 500 corporations.

The recent equity market dislocation of wide swaths of the software industry has provided an opportunity for the Fund to invest in a very well-financed business that appears well positioned to not only survive but potentially thrive in the new A.I. environment. As we often say when talking about companies weathering uncertainty, it starts with the balance sheet. Qualys is undeniably well financed, with no debt and a cash war chest of nearly $700 million, relative to its $3 billion market capitalization. The company has demonstrated a long history of disciplined profitability and methodical platform expansion, balancing revenue growth with high margins and robust operating and free cash flow generation. At recent share prices, current profitability would suggest a free cash flow yield in the double-digits.

While the pace of potential disruption is inherently uncertain, we do take comfort in the fact that large swaths of Qualys customer base come from highly regulated sectors such as finance and healthcare, which maintain regulatory statutes that demand the use of third-party vulnerability management providers such as Qualys.

Further, there is a school of thought that increasing use of agentic A.I. may ultimately increase the need for accuracy, accountability and regulatory compliance of IT environments, which could ultimately support demand for Qualys’ services. The proliferation of generative A.I. agents being rolled out creates more openings for bad actors to exploit insecure software and appears likely to dramatically increase the number of devices that companies will need to monitor to ensure a safe IT environment.

Important Information

This publication does not constitute an offer or solicitation of any transaction in any securities. Any recommendation contained herein may not be suitable for all investors. Information contained in this publication has been obtained from sources we believe to be reliable, but cannot be guaranteed.The information in this portfolio manager letter represents the opinions of the portfolio manager(s) and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results or the actual performance of any fund may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in any such forward-looking statement. data.title: Comfort Systems USA: Long-Term Growth Looks Strong, But Valuation And Technicals Look Stretched (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of Comfort Systems USA (FIX), which I last wrote about in June 2024. The company just reported earnings results for Q1 2026. FIX continues to grow revenue and earnings at a strong, above-average pace.

Comfort Systems USA provides HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and related installations and services to industrial, institutional, and commercial markets. The company's offerings are likely to be needed to replace outdated, less efficient systems and for the long-term growth of new data center build-outs.

FIX's valuation looks lofty at these levels. Plus, the stock reached an extreme overbought level, which may not be sustainable without a profit-taking pullback.

Insights From the Q1 2026 Earnings Results

Comfort Systems USA continues to impress with its stellar above-average revenue and earnings growth. FIX increased revenue by 56.5% yoy to $2.87 billion, beating analysts' estimates by $472.4 million. EPS increased by an impressive 121% yoy to $10.51. Comfort Systems also increased its backlog by 4.3% yoy to a record of $12.5 billion by the end of Q1 2026. This strong backlog should help drive revenue growth going forward.

Organic revenue increased 51% yoy. This shows that the core business is growing at a strong, healthy pace without the need for acquisitions to drive growth.

Revenue growth was achieved in both segments. The electrical segment increased revenue by 88%, while the mechanical segment had a 47% revenue gain. Both segments are benefitting from the tech sector.

FIX is experiencing persistent demand, especially from its tech customers, which is driving this strong revenue growth. Advanced technology customers comprised 56% of Comfort System's total revenue for data center work. Advanced technology is the largest driver of FIX's pipeline and backlog.

The next largest chunk of revenue came from manufacturing at 18.7% of FIX's Q1 2026 revenue. After that was healthcare at 7.7%, government at 4.8%, education at 4.5%, retail/restaurant/entertainment at 2.8%, office buildings at 2.8%, multi-family residential, and the other category comprised the remaining 1.1%.

One key positive factor for Comfort Systems is that revenue is growing faster than expenses. SG&A expenses increased at 49% yoy in Q1 as compared to the revenue growth of 56.5%. If FIX can keep this trend going, the operating margin and net margin can continue to expand.

The company achieved margin expansion across the board in Q1. The gross margin increased from 22% in Q1 2025 to 26% in Q1 2026. The operating margin increased from 11.4% to 16.9%, and the net income margin increased from 9.2% to 12.9% over the same time. These margin gains are driving FIX's strong earnings growth.

The expected growth of data centers to support AI is a long-term growth catalyst for Comfort Systems. The global data center market size is expected to grow at about 11.3% per year to reach over $902 billion by 2033. This growth should act as a steady, long-term catalyst for Comfort Systems. The company's services are likely to be needed for many of these projects.

Comfort Systems provided guidance for organic growth in the mid- to high-20% range for 2026. The company feels this range is at levels that are extremely achievable. This is probably a very conservative estimate, as the phrase 'extremely achievable' was given. The company began the year with a strong 51% increase in organic growth. So, organic growth may wind up being above the company's estimated range for the year.

Valuation

Comfort Systems USA is trading at 43x expected EPS of $40.01 for 2026. This is more than double the sector median forward PE of 20x. It is also more than double the forward PE of 20.5x from where FIX was trading in June 2024 when I last wrote about the company. I realize that the market is giving FIX a premium valuation for its above-average revenue and earnings growth. There is no doubt about Comfort Systems' strong growth story. However, it is not clear that the stock will continue to perform as well as it has at these levels.

The expected EPS of $40.01 would be strong yoy growth of 38.5%. This can be achieved from the expected 28.8% revenue growth. Plus, the margin gains that FIX has been achieving should drive earnings to hit that estimate.

I did mention the PEG ratio as a key metric in my last FIX article. That was based on an estimated average 3- to 5-year earnings growth rate of 15%. The current PEG of 1.2 has the estimated 3- to 5-year earnings growth at 37%. I don't know if that rate will be sustainable for that many years. So, I am cautious about using the PEG ratio to value FIX with that in mind.

Technical Perspective

FIX's monthly chart above shows the stock in an extreme overbought condition with the RSI just below 90. The stock did spend a good amount of time in the overbought zone over the past few years. The MACD is still in bullish mode as the blue line remains above the red signal line. However, the MACD never reached 300 over the past few years.

I doubt that a rise like this is sustainable over the long term without a significant profit-taking pullback. It looks like the stock is due for a pullback similar to the one that occurred in the beginning of 2025. That was when the stock dropped by 50%, from $558 to $280. I don't know whether the stock will drop by a similar amount or not, but a sizeable pullback does look due at these technical levels.

The Risks for the Stock

Inflation could negatively impact the company. Higher costs of labor, utilities, materials, etc. could narrow FIX's margins, leading to less earnings growth. If the Federal Reserve raises interest rates to help lower inflation, it could negatively impact the company's revolving credit facility.

Competition remains an ongoing threat for Comfort Systems. FIX competes with companies of various sizes. The company competes on technical expertise, reputation in the industry, and its financial & operational resources. More intense competition could cut into Comfort System USA's market share, leading to a lower growth rate.

Comfort Systems has a high concentration in a few large customers. The largest customer comprised 12.8% of FIX's total revenue in 2025. Less business from one or more of FIX's largest customers would have a negative impact on revenue and earnings.

Comfort Systems USA's Long-Term Outlook

I like Comfort System USA's strong long-term growth outlook. The multiple-year expected growth of data centers should be a reliable long-term growth driver for the company. Plus, there are likely to be many existing facilities that are in need of ongoing upgrades. Existing and potential new customers will have a need to upgrade outdated facilities with more cost-efficient HVAC and related systems. The company's large backlog should provide about a year's worth of revenue growth.

The issue that I have with Comfort Systems is that the valuation looks stretched. The stock might be priced for perfection at this valuation. The technical setup also points to a possible correction for the stock. Therefore, I am lowering my rating for the stock to 'HOLD'.

data.title: Gaming and Leisure Properties: The Numbers Don't Justify This Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI), I reiterated their Buy rating, highlighting their compelling yield, strong growth guidance, and recent acquisitions.

Following a strong Q1 report, GLPI is still a Buy, with the company continuing to deliver strong results and standing to benefit from their impressive growth plans despite near-term macro pressure, paying an attractive and very sustainable dividend yield on top, and trading at attractive levels that imply a strong margin of safety that already seems to price in significant levels of risk.

Strong Start to 2026

GLPI started 2026 on a strong note, beating the market's FFO and revenue estimates and delivering $297.1 million in AFFO (up 9.2%) during the quarter thanks to rent hikes and acquisitions, with the CEO stating they're in "a very enviable position" thanks to their strong growth pipeline, expecting to deploy ~$1.8 billion by the end of 2027.

Thanks to the strong start, GLPI also hiked its 2026 AFFO guidance, expecting $1.212 billion to $1.223 billion, or about $4.08 to $4.12 per share, compared to $4.06 to $4.11 before and $3.88 in 2025, for a strong 5.28% increase in AFFO per share at midpoint, planning to deploy about $750 million to $800 million into investments in 2026, and expecting to settle $363.3 million of their forward equity on June 1, 2026. Note that their guidance already accounts for roughly 297 million shares outstanding in 2026 as a result (divide the AFFO by the AFFO per share).

Financially, based on GLPI's latest report, we continue to see a solid position for a REIT, with a solid $274.51 million in cash and cash equivalents available and plenty of assets to cover their debt, maintaining their net financial leverage target of 5.0x to 5.5x (currently at 4.96x).

Following their recent debt issuance, GLPI has no near-term maturities (until 2028), and their debt comes at a weighted-average cost of debt of 5.08%, which is not terrible for a company like this, which is after the company's $800 million 2036 notes issued in early March with a 5.625% coupon.

GLPI's dividend stands at about 6.60% today, for about $926.64 million per year, representing a payout ratio of ~76.11% based on their AFFO guidance, leaving plenty of room for future increases and preserving capital for their expansion.

Macro Pressure Could Hurt Plans

Ultimately, the Iran conflict will be the main factor to watch regarding GLPI's near-term performance, as it can affect both the business and the stock significantly, with significant potential to see an inflationary wave that can come even after the conflict is over due to war premiums persisting for the near future given the supply-demand imbalance it caused.

S&P Global currently expects the Fed to delay rate cuts to 2027 due to the near-term inflationary pressure caused by the Iran conflict, meaning we're very likely in for a "higher-for-longer" environment, which is something that goes beyond the Fed Chair's (in this case, Warsh or whoever will get the final approvals) abilities since they're still only 1 out of 12 votes.

In a higher-for-longer interest rate environment, REITs are naturally weak due to their dependency on external capital, and we can see with GLPI how issuing stock comes with a high ~6.6% cost (the dividend), and debt is also far from cheap, with their recent $800 million unsecured senior notes offering due in 2036 coming at a 5.625% interest, limiting the company's ability to enjoy the solid cap rates generated by the industry (8.0% to 8.5% recently).

This can also affect the company's expansion plans, as even if they may have a clear and substantial growth pipeline, the cost of the debt/equity they issue to fund these can increase if the environment gets bad enough. The company's CFO also talked about how they will likely fund the ~$1.8 billion expansion pipeline during their Q1 Earnings Call:

So we sit here today with $275 million of cash that has not been deployed into that run rate of 5x, right? So as that becomes income earning, the leverage ratio will not increase for that portion or for the $363 million of forward equity that we have outstanding. We also have free cash flow to the tune of $230 million or so per year. So we have the majority of that still coming for this year. And then the rest, as we said, we can do either debt or equity depending on what we expect to do. But I still expect us to be at the end of this when all of our transactions are completed, the remaining $1.8 billion is funded, we get full credit for the AFFO that those transactions derive we'll still be at the low end of our 5 to 5.5x guidance or leverage, sorry.

However, I'll also mention the mixed sentiment this industry has in terms of the future due to online gambling (and prediction markets, which they also talked about during their latest Q&A), with some studies showing its potential to complement land-based casinos, allowing operators to expand and attract new people while retaining customers, and I believe this one will ultimately be a matter of the experience they're offering, as gambling from your phone/PC at home is a completely different thing than spending a night in Vegas, for example.

This is somewhat similar to the argument we used to see a lot in the market regarding the death of shopping malls, yet companies like Simon Property Group (SPG) are still doing great regardless of ecommerce's penetration.

Risks Reiterated

Especially given the company's relatively aggressive expansion plans and the potential for the post-conflict inflationary pressure to continue for longer, there's certainly the possibility of seeing them either issue expensive debt or high-yield stock, limiting the potential accretion generated by these investments compared to a better environment.

Meanwhile, this pressure can also affect their tenants since discretionary budgets would fall and these companies' income can be affected in the near term, while iGaming and even prediction markets - although they said they're not "overly concerned about" today - can lead to a scenario where their growth gets limited due to a lack of demand for new supply, at least.

Valuation

For GLPI's updated valuation, starting from an AFFO of $1.22 billion in 2026 based on the midpoint of their new guidance, followed by a 5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their strong growth pipeline and potential for broader macro improvements, with a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

To take into account the higher macro risks, I'll use a higher and conservative discount rate of 11.50%, which should demand a very solid margin of safety for a strong company like this given the industry and limited tenant diversification, with 297 million diluted shares outstanding based on their updated guidance.

This way, we get to an equity value of $15.87 billion, for an intrinsic value of $53.42 per share, which remains above the current $47.31 despite taking into account a high margin of safety.

Conclusion

GLPI remains rated Buy, being backed by a strong business with an attractive and sustainable dividend yield, with growth supported by their solid pipeline and potential macro improvements.

Although there are risks to pay attention to, especially due to the ongoing Iran conflict, the price seems to account for significant levels already, while the company itself continues to deliver strong results even during a high-rate environment.
